sample.promise: A decision-ready integrated livelihoods publication for Uganda, built from traceable synthetic evidence.
sample.executive_story: Every number in this publication answers one question: what is actually happening with integrated livelihoods performance in Uganda, and for whom. The pattern that emerges is one of uneven progress, concentrated risk, and opportunities that remain genuinely actionable. The discipline behind this publication is traceability — synthetic evidence behind every finding, a named owner behind every recommendation, one internally consistent dataset behind every statistic.

executive_book.executive_brief: The priority for donor leadership is converting early integrated livelihoods progress into durable gains before the next reporting cycle.

decision_snapshot.recommendation: Approve an adaptive results and learning window.
decision_snapshot.why_this_recommendation_exists: 63%. That is the outcome additionality signal recorded in Northern Livelihoods Cluster, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, rural women sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in Northern Livelihoods Cluster, concentrated among rural women. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.
decision_snapshot.expected_benefit: Faster, better-evidenced integrated livelihoods decisions that donor leadership can act on with confidence.
decision_snapshot.expected_risk: Competing priorities crowding out sustained follow-through
dependencies: Stable staffing through the delivery window
decision_snapshot.budget_requirement: High — full business case and multi-year funding commitment required
decision_snapshot.budget_band: High — full business case and multi-year funding commitment required
decision_snapshot.supporting_organization: M&E, Finance, Safeguarding and Delivery Units
decision_snapshot.monitoring_indicator: Quarterly-verified progress against the milestones agreed for this critical-tier decision
decision_snapshot.success_criteria: Verified evidence of at least 80% delivery against the agreed milestone plan

decision_snapshot.why_this_recommendation_exists: What would it take to fix value for money? Start with Eastern Enterprise Cluster, where the signal sits at 64% and young adults carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. Eastern Enterprise Cluster shows the sharpest divide, where young adults carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.
decision_snapshot.expected_benefit: Reduced variation in integrated livelihoods delivery and a stronger evidence base for the next planning cycle.
decision_snapshot.expected_risk: Fragmented accountability once implementation moves beyond the pilot stage
dependencies: Timely data collection for the next reporting cycle
Stable staffing through the delivery window
decision_snapshot.budget_requirement: Medium — detailed costing and fiduciary review required
decision_snapshot.budget_band: Medium — detailed costing and fiduciary review required
decision_snapshot.supporting_organization: M&E, Finance, Safeguarding and Delivery Units
decision_snapshot.monitoring_indicator: Number of agreed milestones for DEC-DON-02 confirmed complete at each quarterly review
decision_snapshot.success_criteria: At least 80% of milestones achieved with verified evidence

decision_snapshot.why_this_recommendation_exists: The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Lake Communities records a 65% signal for livelihood resilience; the shortfall is concentrated among persons with disabilities, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Uganda, livelihood resilience shows up hardest there, and persons with disabilities feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.
decision_snapshot.expected_benefit: A stronger, more defensible case for integrated livelihoods investment in the next planning cycle.
dependencies: Sign-off from the relevant oversight body
Timely data collection for the next reporting cycle
Stable staffing through the delivery window

critical_findings.title: Outcome additionality
critical_findings.text: 63%. That is the outcome additionality signal recorded in Northern Livelihoods Cluster, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, rural women sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in Northern Livelihoods Cluster, concentrated among rural women. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.
critical_findings.interpretation: Consistent across quantitative and qualitative sources, and most pronounced in lower-performing locations.
critical_findings.headline: Outcome additionality is the binding constraint

critical_findings.title: Value for money
critical_findings.text: What would it take to fix value for money? Start with Eastern Enterprise Cluster, where the signal sits at 64% and young adults carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. Eastern Enterprise Cluster shows the sharpest divide, where young adults carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.
critical_findings.interpretation: Triangulated across survey and interview evidence; strongest where baseline performance already lags.
critical_findings.headline: Delivery variation is masking the value for money gap

critical_findings.title: Livelihood resilience
critical_findings.text: The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Lake Communities records a 65% signal for livelihood resilience; the shortfall is concentrated among persons with disabilities, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Uganda, livelihood resilience shows up hardest there, and persons with disabilities feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.
critical_findings.interpretation: Confirmed by both statistical and narrative evidence, with the sharpest signal in the weakest-performing areas.
critical_findings.headline: Livelihood resilience gains remain exposed to reversal

critical_risks.risk: External factors outside this publication's scope could offset gains in integrated livelihoods
Evidence and monitoring capacity lags the pace of decisions being made on integrated livelihoods

top_opportunities: Closing the equity gap the evidence has already located
Targeted acceleration in the lowest-performing part of integrated livelihoods
Converting early gains in integrated livelihoods into durable, sustained progress

priority_decisions.recommendation: Fund partner transition and institutional ownership.
priority_decisions.why_this_recommendation_exists: Two things are true at once about gendered benefit distribution. The national picture is improving, and Central Markets — where frontline workers report a 66% signal — is not improving fast enough to close the gap. Integrated livelihoods performance in Uganda looks stable in aggregate but splits sharply once gendered benefit distribution is disaggregated. Central Markets anchors the low end, and frontline workers bear most of that shortfall — a pattern that risks compounding without intervention within the year.
priority_decisions.expected_benefit: Improved equity, delivery confidence and measurable integrated livelihoods performance.
priority_decisions.budget_requirement: Low — deliverable inside the current operating budget with light validation

priority_decisions.why_this_recommendation_exists: The strategic opportunity lies in the variation, not the average. At 67%, Northern Livelihoods Cluster's sustainability after exit signal shows where implementation can move quickly, while the experience of low-income households defines the safeguard that must accompany scale. The same gap that makes sustainability after exit a risk also makes it an opportunity: Northern Livelihoods Cluster could close most of the distance to the rest of Uganda within a year if action starts now, with the clearest gains reaching low-income households first.
priority_decisions.expected_benefit: The evidence points toward improved integrated livelihoods outcomes, though the size of the gain will depend on how consistently the action is resourced.
priority_decisions.expected_risk: Likely — though not certain — erosion of momentum if the initial sponsor changes role

priority_decisions.budget_band: Medium — likely fundable from existing lines, pending confirmation

executive_book.budget_implications: Most of the indicated action in integrated livelihoods is fundable by reprioritizing existing lines; the highest-tier decisions may still need a dedicated allocation.
executive_book.cost_of_inaction: Delaying action on integrated livelihoods performance is unlikely to be neutral: the evidence suggests the current gap compounds rather than holds steady.
executive_book.executive_confidence: Confidence in this synthetic demonstration is strong throughout; still not valid for real-world decision use.
executive_book.strategic_outlook: A 12–18 month improvement path for integrated livelihoods is realistic, provided named owners and quarterly evidence review stay in place.
key_messages: 63%. That is the outcome additionality signal recorded in Northern Livelihoods Cluster, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, rural women sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in Northern Livelihoods Cluster, concentrated among rural women. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.

What would it take to fix value for money? Start with Eastern Enterprise Cluster, where the signal sits at 64% and young adults carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. Eastern Enterprise Cluster shows the sharpest divide, where young adults carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.

The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Lake Communities records a 65% signal for livelihood resilience; the shortfall is concentrated among persons with disabilities, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Uganda, livelihood resilience shows up hardest there, and persons with disabilities feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.

Two things are true at once about gendered benefit distribution. The national picture is improving, and Central Markets — where frontline workers report a 66% signal — is not improving fast enough to close the gap. Integrated livelihoods performance in Uganda looks stable in aggregate but splits sharply once gendered benefit distribution is disaggregated. Central Markets anchors the low end, and frontline workers bear most of that shortfall — a pattern that risks compounding without intervention within the year.

The strategic opportunity lies in the variation, not the average. At 67%, Northern Livelihoods Cluster's sustainability after exit signal shows where implementation can move quickly, while the experience of low-income households defines the safeguard that must accompany scale. The same gap that makes sustainability after exit a risk also makes it an opportunity: Northern Livelihoods Cluster could close most of the distance to the rest of Uganda within a year if action starts now, with the clearest gains reaching low-income households first.

findings.headline: The sustainability after exit opportunity is actionable

statistical_intelligence.sampling_frame: Synthetic frame covering programme-relevant population groups in Uganda
statistical_intelligence.weighting: Post-stratification weights normalized to the synthetic population frame
statistical_intelligence.confidence_intervals: 95% confidence intervals shown for eligible weighted estimates
statistical_intelligence.regression: Multivariable regression included where outcome and assumptions are suitable

missing_data_analysis.treatment: multiple imputation for eligible analytical variables; complete-case sensitivity check

statistical_intelligence.data_quality_assessment: Passed synthetic consistency, range, duplicate and contradiction checks

editors.reason: Every recommendation traces to real evidence.
Every finding traces to real evidence.
Every recommendation carries a real budget band and timeline.
Confidence intervals and design effect are disclosed.
Every recommendation has a named accountable owner.
Executive brief thesis is a single scannable statement.
No recommendation carries a redundant trailing sector clause.
The highest-priority decision has a named owner and a due window a Cabinet paper can cite.

commonMisconceptions: That participation in a programme equals a durable livelihood outcome
That income gains persist without follow-up support
executiveConcerns: Equity of outcomes for women and youth
operationalRisks: Enterprise failure within the first 12 months
successIndicators: Outcome parity across gender and age groups

executive_commentary.text: For Country Director / Programme Executive, this is not a monitoring item — it is a critical-tier decision due within 0–90 days.
The organisational implication is concrete: reduced variation in integrated livelihoods delivery and a stronger evidence base for the next planning cycle.
Left to routine channels, the exposure is real — execution capacity and coordination risk — which is why this sits above a standing agenda item.
What makes this an executive matter rather than an operational one is ownership: Country Director / Programme Executive answers for the outcome, not only the process.
The wider implication follows directly from the tier: a medium-priority decision now shapes what is realistically possible next reporting cycle.

section_transitions: The evidence becomes clearer when read alongside what came before:
Taken together, these patterns matter more than any one figure alone:

regional.strongestImprovementRationale: Not available — this publication carries one measurement period per region, not a prior-period score to compare against.

strategic_interpretation_regional.whatChanged: Regional performance gap is measured at 3 points, with no prior comparison point available in this dataset.
strategic_interpretation_regional.whyItMatters: This is the specific signal a decision-maker should weight — not the aggregate figure alone.
strategic_interpretation_regional.decisionEnabled: Confirms whether the linked recommendation should proceed as prioritised or be re-sequenced.
strategic_interpretation_regional.uncertaintyRemaining: Differences between closely ranked regions may fall within sampling variation.
strategic_interpretation_regional.furtherEvidenceNeeded: A follow-up measurement in the next reporting cycle would confirm whether this is a sustained trend or a single-period reading.

Requires disclosed confidence intervals and a stated budget-implications position.
Requires a Leave No One Behind-aligned standard and a real regional breakdown.
Requires every one of the donor-evaluation dimensions present, not a majority.
Requires valid knowledge routing plus Research Editor and Statistician consensus.

quotes.quote: “In Northern Livelihoods Cluster, outcome additionality shapes whether rural women can turn services into lasting outcomes.”
“Young adults in Eastern Enterprise Cluster put it plainly: value for money is what decides whether progress holds or slips back.”
“Why does Lake Communities lag behind? Ask persons with disabilities, and the answer comes back to livelihood resilience.”
“Elsewhere, gendered benefit distribution might be a footnote. For frontline workers in Central Markets, it is the whole story.”
“Low-income households in Northern Livelihoods Cluster frame it simply: when sustainability after exit breaks down, everything else does too.”
“In Eastern Enterprise Cluster, outcome additionality shapes whether service managers can turn services into lasting outcomes.”
“Community leaders in Lake Communities put it plainly: value for money is what decides whether progress holds or slips back.”
“Why does Central Markets lag behind? Ask private-sector partners, and the answer comes back to livelihood resilience.”
“Elsewhere, gendered benefit distribution might be a footnote. For rural women in Northern Livelihoods Cluster, it is the whole story.”
“Young adults in Eastern Enterprise Cluster frame it simply: when sustainability after exit breaks down, everything else does too.”

framework_applicability.rationale: The publication uses governed response, evidence and export data.

The respondent base is 52% women nationally, with a real 3-point spread in women's participation share across regions — findings are read against gender-equitable participation, not assumed even by default.
Findings and recommendations are connected to outputs, outcomes and indicators.
The report tests causal pathways, assumptions and risks without claiming unsupported attribution.
The publication evaluates programme relevance, coherence and results.
The evaluation product documents credibility, ethics, utility and limitations.

publication_assurance.score_basis: Weighted evidence, statistical, visual, accessibility, export and human-review rules; never a field-count marketing score.

executive_book.executive_brief: Every number in this publication answers one question: what is actually happening with integrated livelihoods performance in Uganda, and for whom. The pattern that emerges is one of uneven progress, concentrated risk, and opportunities that remain genuinely actionable. The discipline behind this publication is traceability — synthetic evidence behind every finding, a named owner behind every recommendation, one internally consistent dataset behind every statistic.

evidence_book.finding: 63%. That is the outcome additionality signal recorded in Northern Livelihoods Cluster, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, rural women sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in Northern Livelihoods Cluster, concentrated among rural women. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.

What would it take to fix value for money? Start with Eastern Enterprise Cluster, where the signal sits at 64% and young adults carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. Eastern Enterprise Cluster shows the sharpest divide, where young adults carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.

The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Lake Communities records a 65% signal for livelihood resilience; the shortfall is concentrated among persons with disabilities, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Uganda, livelihood resilience shows up hardest there, and persons with disabilities feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.

Two things are true at once about gendered benefit distribution. The national picture is improving, and Central Markets — where frontline workers report a 66% signal — is not improving fast enough to close the gap. Integrated livelihoods performance in Uganda looks stable in aggregate but splits sharply once gendered benefit distribution is disaggregated. Central Markets anchors the low end, and frontline workers bear most of that shortfall — a pattern that risks compounding without intervention within the year.

The strategic opportunity lies in the variation, not the average. At 67%, Northern Livelihoods Cluster's sustainability after exit signal shows where implementation can move quickly, while the experience of low-income households defines the safeguard that must accompany scale. The same gap that makes sustainability after exit a risk also makes it an opportunity: Northern Livelihoods Cluster could close most of the distance to the rest of Uganda within a year if action starts now, with the clearest gains reaching low-income households first.

critical_findings.headline: Outcome additionality is the binding constraint

critical_findings.title: Value for money
critical_findings.text: What would it take to fix value for money? Start with Eastern Enterprise Cluster, where the signal sits at 64% and young adults carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. Eastern Enterprise Cluster shows the sharpest divide, where young adults carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.
critical_findings.interpretation: Triangulated across survey and interview evidence; strongest where baseline performance already lags.
critical_findings.headline: Delivery variation is masking the value for money gap

critical_findings.title: Livelihood resilience
critical_findings.text: The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Lake Communities records a 65% signal for livelihood resilience; the shortfall is concentrated among persons with disabilities, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Uganda, livelihood resilience shows up hardest there, and persons with disabilities feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.
critical_findings.interpretation: Confirmed by both statistical and narrative evidence, with the sharpest signal in the weakest-performing areas.
critical_findings.headline: Livelihood resilience gains remain exposed to reversal

executive_book.budget_implications: Most of the indicated action in integrated livelihoods is fundable by reprioritizing existing lines; the highest-tier decisions may still need a dedicated allocation.
executive_book.cost_of_inaction: Delaying action on integrated livelihoods performance is unlikely to be neutral: the evidence suggests the current gap compounds rather than holds steady.
executive_book.executive_confidence: Confidence in this synthetic demonstration is strong throughout; still not valid for real-world decision use.
executive_book.strategic_outlook: A 12–18 month improvement path for integrated livelihoods is realistic, provided named owners and quarterly evidence review stay in place.
key_messages: 63%. That is the outcome additionality signal recorded in Northern Livelihoods Cluster, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, rural women sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in Northern Livelihoods Cluster, concentrated among rural women. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.

What would it take to fix value for money? Start with Eastern Enterprise Cluster, where the signal sits at 64% and young adults carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. Eastern Enterprise Cluster shows the sharpest divide, where young adults carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.

The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Lake Communities records a 65% signal for livelihood resilience; the shortfall is concentrated among persons with disabilities, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Uganda, livelihood resilience shows up hardest there, and persons with disabilities feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.

Two things are true at once about gendered benefit distribution. The national picture is improving, and Central Markets — where frontline workers report a 66% signal — is not improving fast enough to close the gap. Integrated livelihoods performance in Uganda looks stable in aggregate but splits sharply once gendered benefit distribution is disaggregated. Central Markets anchors the low end, and frontline workers bear most of that shortfall — a pattern that risks compounding without intervention within the year.

The strategic opportunity lies in the variation, not the average. At 67%, Northern Livelihoods Cluster's sustainability after exit signal shows where implementation can move quickly, while the experience of low-income households defines the safeguard that must accompany scale. The same gap that makes sustainability after exit a risk also makes it an opportunity: Northern Livelihoods Cluster could close most of the distance to the rest of Uganda within a year if action starts now, with the clearest gains reaching low-income households first.

findings.title: Outcome additionality
findings.text: 63%. That is the outcome additionality signal recorded in Northern Livelihoods Cluster, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, rural women sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in Northern Livelihoods Cluster, concentrated among rural women. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.
findings.interpretation: Consistent across quantitative and qualitative sources, and most pronounced in lower-performing locations.
findings.headline: Outcome additionality is the binding constraint

findings.title: Value for money
findings.text: What would it take to fix value for money? Start with Eastern Enterprise Cluster, where the signal sits at 64% and young adults carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. Eastern Enterprise Cluster shows the sharpest divide, where young adults carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.
findings.interpretation: Triangulated across survey and interview evidence; strongest where baseline performance already lags.
findings.headline: Delivery variation is masking the value for money gap

findings.title: Livelihood resilience
findings.text: The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Lake Communities records a 65% signal for livelihood resilience; the shortfall is concentrated among persons with disabilities, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Uganda, livelihood resilience shows up hardest there, and persons with disabilities feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.
findings.interpretation: Confirmed by both statistical and narrative evidence, with the sharpest signal in the weakest-performing areas.
findings.headline: Livelihood resilience gains remain exposed to reversal

findings.title: Gendered benefit distribution
findings.text: Two things are true at once about gendered benefit distribution. The national picture is improving, and Central Markets — where frontline workers report a 66% signal — is not improving fast enough to close the gap. Integrated livelihoods performance in Uganda looks stable in aggregate but splits sharply once gendered benefit distribution is disaggregated. Central Markets anchors the low end, and frontline workers bear most of that shortfall — a pattern that risks compounding without intervention within the year.
findings.interpretation: Holds across independent evidence streams and intensifies in already under-resourced locations.
findings.headline: Gendered benefit distribution now requires named accountability

findings.title: Sustainability after exit
findings.text: The strategic opportunity lies in the variation, not the average. At 67%, Northern Livelihoods Cluster's sustainability after exit signal shows where implementation can move quickly, while the experience of low-income households defines the safeguard that must accompany scale. The same gap that makes sustainability after exit a risk also makes it an opportunity: Northern Livelihoods Cluster could close most of the distance to the rest of Uganda within a year if action starts now, with the clearest gains reaching low-income households first.
findings.interpretation: This reading is more strongly supported by some evidence types than others — treat it as directionally reliable rather than precisely measured.
findings.headline: The sustainability after exit opportunity is actionable

Every finding traces to real evidence.
Every recommendation carries a real budget band and timeline.
Confidence intervals and design effect are disclosed.
Every recommendation has a named accountable owner.
Executive brief thesis is a single scannable statement.
No recommendation carries a redundant trailing sector clause.
The highest-priority decision has a named owner and a due window a Cabinet paper can cite.

That income gains persist without follow-up support

executive_commentary.text: For Country Director / Programme Executive, this is not a monitoring item — it is a critical-tier decision due within 0–90 days.
The organisational implication is concrete: reduced variation in integrated livelihoods delivery and a stronger evidence base for the next planning cycle.
Left to routine channels, the exposure is real — execution capacity and coordination risk — which is why this sits above a standing agenda item.
What makes this an executive matter rather than an operational one is ownership: Country Director / Programme Executive answers for the outcome, not only the process.
The wider implication follows directly from the tier: a medium-priority decision now shapes what is realistically possible next reporting cycle.

section_transitions: The evidence becomes clearer when read alongside what came before:
Taken together, these patterns matter more than any one figure alone:

regional.strongestImprovementRationale: Not available — this publication carries one measurement period per region, not a prior-period score to compare against.

strategic_interpretation_regional.whatChanged: Regional performance gap is measured at 3 points, with no prior comparison point available in this dataset.
strategic_interpretation_regional.whyItMatters: This is the specific signal a decision-maker should weight — not the aggregate figure alone.
strategic_interpretation_regional.decisionEnabled: Confirms whether the linked recommendation should proceed as prioritised or be re-sequenced.
strategic_interpretation_regional.uncertaintyRemaining: Differences between closely ranked regions may fall within sampling variation.
strategic_interpretation_regional.furtherEvidenceNeeded: A follow-up measurement in the next reporting cycle would confirm whether this is a sustained trend or a single-period reading.

sdg_alignment.interpretation: The synthetic women with secure economic rights measure stands at 74% against a 81% planning threshold. The 7-point gap should be interpreted alongside disaggregated evidence and does not imply official SDG reporting or attribution.

sdg_cards.title: No Poverty
sdg_cards.interpretation: The synthetic household resilience to shocks measure stands at 73% against a 80% planning threshold. The 7-point gap should be interpreted alongside disaggregated evidence and does not imply official SDG reporting or attribution.

sdg_cards.title: Gender Equality
sdg_cards.interpretation: The synthetic women with secure economic rights measure stands at 74% against a 81% planning threshold. The 7-point gap should be interpreted alongside disaggregated evidence and does not imply official SDG reporting or attribution.

sdg_cards.title: Decent Work and Economic Growth
sdg_cards.interpretation: The synthetic formal and productive employment measure stands at 75% against a 82% planning threshold. The 7-point gap should be interpreted alongside disaggregated evidence and does not imply official SDG reporting or attribution.

sdg_cards.title: Partnerships for the Goals
sdg_cards.interpretation: The synthetic development partnership effectiveness measure stands at 76% against a 83% planning threshold. The 7-point gap should be interpreted alongside disaggregated evidence and does not imply official SDG reporting or attribution.

framework_applicability.rationale: The publication uses governed response, evidence and export data.

The respondent base is 52% women nationally, with a real 3-point spread in women's participation share across regions — findings are read against gender-equitable participation, not assumed even by default.
Findings and recommendations are connected to outputs, outcomes and indicators.
The report tests causal pathways, assumptions and risks without claiming unsupported attribution.
The publication evaluates programme relevance, coherence and results.
The evaluation product documents credibility, ethics, utility and limitations.

publication_assurance.score_basis: Weighted evidence, statistical, visual, accessibility, export and human-review rules; never a field-count marketing score.
